coredata.dc:title: Uv–vis spectroscopy for simple chloride content analysis in edible oil using charged amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots fluorophore reagent
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Abu Bakar, Mohd Hafiz
Azeman, Nur Hidayah
Mobarak, Nadhratun Naiim
Nazri, Nur Afifah Ahmad
Daniyal, Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim Mohd
Othman, Muhammad Qayyum
Abdullah, Fairuz
Bakar, Ahmad Ashrif A.
coredata.dc:description: The utilization of UV–Vis spectroscopy with amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQD) as a positive fluorophore reagent for chloride sensing in oil marks a notable advancement in analytical spectroscopy chemistry. This approach streamlines the detection process by eliminating the need for lengthy procedures and pretreatment steps typically associated with chloride detection in edible oil. By incorporating NCQD in chloride detection within the oil matrix, the wavelength analysis transitions from the UV to the visible region. This shift eliminates interference from oil matrix interactions, ensuring more accurate results. Molecular analysis of NCQD reveals significant shifts in its Fourier Transformation Infrared and photoluminescence spectroscopy peaks due to interaction with chloride in edible oil. It has two impressive sensitivity ranges spanning from 0.1–1.0 to 1.0–8.0 ppm, with a value of −0.4656 au. ppm−1 (R2 = 0.998) and −0.0361 au. ppm−1 (R2 = 0.931), respectively, the technique meets regulatory standards while achieving a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 ppm. This places it on par with conventional methods and commercial sensors. The NCQD-UV–Vis spectroscopy method not only enhances the efficiency and accuracy of chloride detection but also holds promise for various industrial applications requiring simple and precise monitoring of chloride levels in oil samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Spectroscopy
Edible oil
Ion detection and reagent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: NCQD as reagent to detect chloride in edible oil.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author acknowledges the Research University Grant (GUP), grant number GUP-2023-043, funded by the Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM), Malaysia.

The authors would like to thank the Photonics Laboratory of the Department of Electrical, Electronic & Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment, Faculty of Science and Technology, and the Centre for Research and Instrument Management (CRIM), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM) for all amenities provided. Also, this work was supported by Tenaga Nasional Berhad (TNB) and UNITEN through the BOLD Refresh Postdoctoral Fellowships under Grant J510050002-IC-6 BOLDREFRESH2025-Centre of Excellence.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The interaction and extraction process of NCQD reagent.
(a) UV–Vis absorbance, (b) photoluminescence excitation and (c) photoluminescence emission spectra of NCQD.
Surface morphology of NCQD using HRTEM showing the structure of a few dots of NCQD with average size of 5.903 nm.

(a) FTIR spectrum of NCQD before (black) and after interaction (red) with chloride ion in edible oil, (b) Photoluminescence excitation, and (c) photoluminescence emission of NCQD after interaction with chloride in edible oil.
UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of NCQD with the presence of chloride ion in solution.
UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of NCQD with the presence of chloride ion in solution.
Comparison technique for detection of chloride in EOM.

head.ce:title: Uv–vis spectroscopy for simple chloride content analysis in edible oil using charged amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots fluorophore reagent

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilization of UV–Vis spectroscopy with amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQD) as a positive fluorophore reagent for chloride sensing in oil marks a notable advancement in analytical spectroscopy chemistry. This approach streamlines the detection process by eliminating the need for lengthy procedures and pretreatment steps typically associated with chloride detection in edible oil. By incorporating NCQD in chloride detection within the oil matrix, the wavelength analysis transitions from the UV to the visible region. This shift eliminates interference from oil matrix interactions, ensuring more accurate results. Molecular analysis of NCQD reveals significant shifts in its Fourier Transformation Infrared and photoluminescence spectroscopy peaks due to interaction with chloride in edible oil. It has two impressive sensitivity ranges spanning from 0.1–1.0 to 1.0–8.0 ppm, with a value of −0.4656 au. ppm−1 (R2 = 0.998) and −0.0361 au. ppm−1 (R2 = 0.931), respectively, the technique meets regulatory standards while achieving a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 ppm. This places it on par with conventional methods and commercial sensors. The NCQD-UV–Vis spectroscopy method not only enhances the efficiency and accuracy of chloride detection but also holds promise for various industrial applications requiring simple and precise monitoring of chloride levels in oil samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the UN, by 2050, the world population is expected to surpass 9 billion. This increase will lead to a 70 percent rise in food production demands, as stated by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations [1,2]. The rapid population growth results in problems such as heightened competition and the unsustainable use of land and water resources for agriculture, disregarding food safety. Consequently, there will be an increased need for safe food. Particularly in Europe and America, the edible oil industry faces strict regulations concerning the levels of 3-monochloro-1,2-propanediol (3-MCPD) carcinogenic ester compound and total chlorine in its products, indicating significant potential markets. Therefore, the industry must adopt sustainable practices to ensure safe and healthy food for the expanding population.

Crude palm oil (CPO) is a key product in the edible oil industry, and there are growing concerns within the industry regarding the declining quality of CPO, primarily attributed to its elevated chlorine content [3]. The existence of organic and inorganic chlorine compounds in the CPO during the refinery process can lead to the formation of the carcinogenic 3-MCPD ester. This compound has been designated by the International Agency for Research on Cancer as falling into either Group 2A or 2B, indicating its potential as a carcinogen in humans [4,5]. The formation of 3-MCPD ester is directly proportional to the levels of fatty acids and chlorine content in the oil during the refinery process, typically carried out at temperatures between 180 and 270 °C [4,6]. The presence of ionic-bound chloride and organic chlorine is a key criterion for forming 3-MCPD.

Therefore, it is crucial to employ a method capable of monitoring the chlorine levels in CPO throughout the milling process before the refinery process. The standard techniques for assessing chlorine concentration in CPO before refining typically involve the use of a total chlorine analyzer (utilizing pyrolysis), nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy and ion chromatography [3,7–13]. Although these methods effectively monitor the chlorine content in CPO, they are time-consuming and costly. They may not be practical for every mill, primarily in rural areas. Consequently, a simple, cost-effective sensor is necessary to monitor the chloride content in CPO during the milling process.

The development of a UV–Vis spectroscopy method utilizing modified amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQD) for chloride detection shows promise. This technique exhibits high sensitivity to chlorine and simplicity, facilitating easy deployment at palm oil mills. Implementing this approach enables the creation of a simple, cost-effective detection method to monitor chloride content in CPO during the milling process prior to its transportation to the refinery. Such monitoring aids in ensuring the production of safe, high-quality palm oil products while providing an early mitigation of 3-MCPD formation.

The utilization of NCQD as a reagent holds promise, mainly due to its optical properties that allow for analysis through UV–Vis light absorption. NCQD is a fluorophore, capable of both exciting and emitting light based on its interaction with the targeted analyte [14,15]. This multifunctional compound exhibits diverse applications, serving as a sensing material in various sensor technologies, acting as an active substance for visualizing plant tissue, boosting fuel cell efficiency, and finding utility in membrane fabrication processes [16–20]. Furthermore, research indicates that NCQD, particularly in its positively charged form and consist of variety of chemical functional group, exhibits potential for detecting chloride within the edible oil matrix (EOM). This is particularly significant given that EOM typically absorbs light in the UV region, thus limiting detection capabilities. However, leveraging NCQD can extend the detection range into the visible region, expanding the scope of analytes that can be effectively detected.

The current study introduces a novel approach employing UV–Vis spectroscopy utilizing NCQD as a reagent for detecting chloride ions in EOM. This method offers the advantage of simplicity and rapidity in detection, facilitating efficient analysis of samples. Different interactions between the analyte and NCQD are anticipated to enable the successful detection of chloride ions in EOM. This research holds significant potential as a valuable contribution to alignment with the UN Sustainable Development Goals, explicitly targeting Goal 2: Zero Hunger and Goal 3: Good Health and Well-being, thereby addressing critical global challenges related to food security and public health.

Citric acid, polyethyleneimine, isopropyl alcohol, sodium chloride, polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan, and acetone were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Ethylenediamine, metal salts, and ammonia solution (30 %) were purchased from R&M Chemicals, and total chlorophyll was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd.

The functionalized CQD was synthesized using two steps of hydrothermal methods according to the previously reported procedure [16]. The citric acid (1.0507 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and 335 Microliter of ethylenediamine was added to the mixture and heated at 200 °C for 12 h using a Teflon-line autoclave. Upon the reaction, the CQD solution was naturally cooled at room temperature. Further, 1 mL of polyethyleneimine solution was added, and the resulting mixture was heated again for 5 h at 80 °C. The solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min and filtered using a 0.22-µm membrane filter. Finally, the supernatant solution was collected and dialyzed using a dialysis bag for a day. The NCQD solution was obtained and kept for further characterization.

In a volumetric flask, a 600-ppm chloride stock was prepared by dissolving sodium chloride in minimal deionized (DI) water. Subsequently, the solution was blended with isopropyl alcohol (IPA) until reaching the meniscus level. A few milliliters of the chloride stock solution were injected into the refined palm oil matrix and stirred overnight at 50 °C to achieve a homogeneous, yellowish-cloudy solution. Following this, a series of chloride ion solutions in a refined oil matrix were prepared from this stock solution using a standard dilution method. The interaction and extraction process are illustrated in Fig. 1 . Subsequently, the extracted NCQD was analyzed using the UV–Vis spectroscopy method.

The FTIR analysis played a crucial role in molecularly characterizing the synthesized NCQD and investigating its interaction with chloride ions. Fig. 2 illustrates the FTIR spectra, where the NCQD spectra reveals a distinct fingerprint peak at 1647 cm−1, indicative of the bending mode for the N–H peak of primary amine. Additionally, peaks at 2943 and 2851 cm−1 signify the stretch for sp2 and sp3 CH, while 1470 cm−1 represents the stretching for CH. Notably, a broad peak at 3278 cm−1 indicates the OH stretching vibration and 3354 cm−1 suggests the presence of NH groups [21,22]. The emergence of these peaks confirms the successful synthesis of NCQD, aligning with the finding through experimental procedures established by prior research. The previous synthesis report has discussed the comparison between CQD and NCQD. It detailed the modification of CQD by introducing the amine group into the structure, which is in accordance with the objectives of our current work [17,23].

Meanwhile, Fig. 3 shows the optical properties of NCQD. Fig. 3 (a) represents the UV–Vis absorption of NCQD, revealing two distinct absorption peaks spiked at 248 nm and 343 nm when exposed to UV–Vis light. The peak observed at 248 nm is attributed to the presence of a carbon double bond containing π and π*, as documented in the FTIR analysis above. When the C=C bond in NCQD is exposed to light, the electron absorbs photon energy in the π orbital, becoming excited to the π* energy level [23,24]. Additionally, the notable spike in UV–Vis absorption at 343 nm may stem from the transition of an electron from a non-bonding orbital to the σ* (n - σ*) orbital in nitrogen (N), as indicated in the FTIR spectra. This transition absorbs lower-energy UV light due to the smaller energy band gap between these two orbitals producing a lower absorption peak at 343 nm [25]. In Fig. 3(b), the photoluminescence excitation of NCQD under UV light exposure is depicted at 347 nm in photoluminescence and generating a maximum emission peak at 583 nm (Fig. 3(c)), resulting in a vivid green-yellowish color. These findings are similar to the previous report proving the effectiveness of NCQD synthesis through hydro-thermal reaction [23].

In Fig. 4 , the surface morphology analysis of the synthesized NCQD using HRTEM is depicted, revealing the presence of NCQD particles sized less than 10 nm. This observation underscores the efficacy of the thermal cleaving method employed during NCQD synthesis, corroborating findings from prior studies as described in prior studies [23]. In this method, the NCQD were subjected to filtration using an osmosis process with filter membranes to refine their size distribution. This filtration step successfully eliminated any residual reactants and smaller NCQD particles, facilitating the isolation of NCQD of the desired size. The HRTEM images was analyzed using statistical histogram analysis to determine the uniformity of NCQD distribution [26]. The analysis yielded a mean NCQD size of 5.903 nm with a standard deviation of 2.77 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NCQD as reagent to detect chloride in edible oil.
ce:section.ce:para: Chemical modification of CDs to NCQD through hydrothermal reaction yields a positively charged NCQD as reported in Zeta potential previously reported by Nazri et al. in 2022 and Qian et al. in 2018 [16,17].

Furthermore, NCQD has been reported to exhibit hydrophilic properties [19]. These characteristics are essential for facilitating interaction with chloride ions and preventing interaction with the oil matrix. Such properties can be harnessed as a potential sensing material to interact with negatively charged chloride ions, especially within oil matrices. The research on NCQD as the reagent to identify changes in chloride ion concentration in edible oil matrix has been done using the UV–Vis absorbance technique, as depicted in the setup and experimental procedure in Fig. 1.

Fig. 5 (a) compares the FTIR spectrum of pure NCQD with the extracted NCQD after the interaction with chloride ions in the EOM. Based on this FTIR spectrum, there is no apparent difference between the two, except for the emergence of a new peak at 1700 cm−1, and most of the peaks from the extracted NCQD have undergone a redshift after the interaction. The appearance of the new peak at 1700 cm−1 signifies the mode of vibration of C=O due to the introduction of dissolved carbonyl moieties from the EOM into the NCQD solution during the extraction process. Meanwhile, the positive charge of the overall NCQD interacts with the chloride ions in the EOM, causing significant effects on most of the bonding in NCQD. This interaction increases the bond length between two atoms, causing them to absorb lower energy at lower wave numbers than the pure NCQD before the extraction process [21].

Similarly, the photoluminescence analysis depicted in Fig. 5(b) demonstrates a shift in the maximum excitation wavelength for the extracted NCQD, transitioning from 347 nm to 351 nm. This shift suggests an interaction between differently charged NCQD and Cl-, resulting in a reduced energy requirement for excitation in the photoluminescence analysis. Likewise, the maximum intensity (Fig. 5(c)) of the emission wavelength for the extracted NCQD has shifted to a lower energy wavelength, moving from 583 nm to 588 nm. Based on these findings, it is plausible to infer that NCQD may interact with chloride ions in EOM.

Fig. 6 displays the UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of the extracted NCQD following its interaction with a chloride ion emulsion in EOM. The high absorbance value observed in the UV–Vis spectra can indeed be attributed to the excellent soluble colloidal properties of NCQD, which allow them to disperse effectively in the solution. This property enables NCQD to scatter light, leading to a decrease in the intensity of transmitted light [18,27,28]. The absorbance reveals a broad absorption profile of NCQD ranging from 250 nm to 600 nm, signifying interaction within NCQD after exposure to EOM with varying chloride ion concentrations. This is confirmed by the difference in the absorption patterns of NCQD pre- and post-interaction. Initially, two peaks were observed with wavelengths below 400 nm. However, post-interaction, a new peak emerged at 480 nm, indicating the extract's absorption of the visible blue region. This shift in absorption characteristics underscores the dynamic nature of NCQD when subjected to different chloride ion concentrations within the EOM, as illustrated by the changes in its UV–Vis absorbance spectrum. The shoulder peak observed in the absorbance spectrum of the extracted NCQD at 380 nm demonstrates a consistent and noteworthy trend of decrease with an increase in the concentration of chloride ions, indicating a relationship between the presence of chloride ions and the observed changes in the NCQD absorbance spectrum. Therefore, the sensitivity analysis has been done at this wavelength using a standard curve of extracted NCQD absorbance versus chloride ion concentration in EOM, as depicted in Fig. 7 . The study of how NCQD and chloride ions in EOM interact has revealed an unexpected finding about turbidity in the solution. Absorbance values above 600 nm indicate the presence of turbidity, which could elevate the baseline absorbance in UV–Vis spectra [29]. This turbidity might be caused by the solvent interacting with the other compound in EOM [30].

Sensitivity stands as an important performance metric for any developed method. The sensitivity of the resultant sensor was calculated to assess the effectiveness of UV–Vis analysis utilizing NCQD as a reagent. Fig. 7 illustrates the sensitivity analysis of UV–Vis-NQCD absorbance across varying chloride concentrations ranging from 0 to 15 ppm. It illustrates the absorbance of extracted NCQD reduced with increasing chloride ion concentration in the EOM. Through linear regression, there are two slopes of sensitivity. The first slope produced a sensitivity of −0.4656 a.u. ppm−1 with a correlation (R2) 0.998 derived from 0 ppm to 1 ppm. The notable sensitivity of NCQD may be attributed to its high charge density, facilitating rapid interaction with negatively charged Cl- ions in this concentration region. Subsequently, the method yielded a second slope for chloride detection ranging from 1 ppm to 8 ppm with R2 = 0.9307, with a sensitivity of −0.0361 a.u. ppm−1. This decrease in sensitivity could stem from the weakened practical collision between NCQD and chloride ions in the EOM, hindering their interaction [31]. Such reduction in effective collision may be attributed to lessening readily available positively charged sites of NCQD for chloride ion interaction in this second slope region.

The performance and complexity of the method developed in this study are compared with previously reported approaches for detecting chlorine in EOM. Table 1 summarizes the comparison of sensitivity, LOD, and detection range. In this study, the utilization of NCQD as a reagent for detecting chloride in EOM demonstrates a comparable limit of detection for chloride ions compared to both commercial analyzers and established methods, detecting levels as low as 0.1 ppm up to 8.0 ppm of chloride ions in EOM. Furthermore, this developed method reduced the procedure and sample pretreatment compared to other methods, which often necessitate extensive sample preparation for qualitative identification. The interaction and extraction method employed here requires less time, direct method and eliminate the need for additional chemical reagents to remove chemical interferences.

In conclusion, the integration of UV–Vis spectroscopy with NCQD as a fluorophore reagent represents a groundbreaking development in analytical chemistry, particularly for chloride sensing in edible oil. By utilizing UV–Vis-NCQD, the requirement for sample pretreatment procedures is bypassed, thereby significantly streamlining the detection process. This advancement not only simplifies the analytical workflow but also extends the spectral range into the visible spectrum, mitigating edible oil matrix interference issues commonly encountered in conventional UV–Vis techniques. The technique demonstrates impressive sensitivity, with a detection range spanning from 0.1 to 8.0 ppm, meeting regulatory standards. Moreover, the NCQD-UV–Vis spectroscopy method boasts a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 ppm, comparable to both standard methods and commercial sensors. This innovation not only enhances the efficiency and accuracy of chloride detection but also offers promising implications for various industrial applications requiring simple and precise monitoring of chloride levels in oil samples. Furthermore, with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 ppm, UV–Vis-NCQD emerges as a competitive alternative to established methods, offering comparable performance while eliminating complexity. While this study provides valuable insights into the interaction between NCQD and chloride oil, accurately determining binding affinity remains challenging for additional interaction analysis. Future research employing techniques such as isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) or surface plasmon resonance (SPR) is necessary to elucidate this aspect further. These discoveries not only underscore the significant potential of the method to transform the detection of ion/carcinogen compounds in edible oil, offering enhanced efficiency, precision, and adaptability across various industrial settings, but also signify a revolution in the application of carbon dots for detecting chemical compounds within the oil matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the author used ChatGPT to improve language and readability, with caution. After using this tool/service, the author reviewed and edited the content as needed and took full responsibility for the content of the publication.
The author acknowledges the Research University Grant (GUP), grant number GUP-2023-043, funded by the Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM), Malaysia.

Mohd Hafiz Abu Bakar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Nur Hidayah Azeman: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Nadhratun Naiim Mobarak: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Nur Afifah Ahmad Nazri: Investigation. Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim Mohd Daniyal: Writing – review & editing. Muhammad Qayyum Othman: Investigation. Fairuz Abdullah: . Ahmad Ashrif A. Bakar: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Photonics Laboratory of the Department of Electrical, Electronic & Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment, Faculty of Science and Technology, and the Centre for Research and Instrument Management (CRIM), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM) for all amenities provided. Also, this work was supported by Tenaga Nasional Berhad (TNB) and UNITEN through the BOLD RefreshPostdoctoral Fellowships under Grant J510050002-IC-6 BOLDREFRESH2025-Centre of Excellence.
